title: How the body creates fibromyalgia symptoms from unresolved trauma
author: Kalesh

metaDescription:
Your body is not broken. It's protecting you. 

 I remember the first time a doctor told me my pain was "all in my head." I wanted to scream. Not because...
htmlContent:
Your body is not broken. It's protecting you.

I remember the first time a doctor told me my pain was "all in my head." I wanted to scream. Not because she was wrong, but because she was right in a way she didn't understand. The pain was real. The fatigue was real. The burning, the stabbing, the endless ache that made me want to crawl out of my own skin. That was all real. But she didn't know how to look at what was actually causing it. Here's the thing. Modern medicine treats the body and mind as separate. They're not. And when you've lived through trauma, your body doesn't forget. It keeps score. And sometimes, it keeps score with fibromyalgia.

Let me be direct with you. If you're reading this, you've probably been told you're fine. That your tests come back normal. That there's nothing physically wrong. And you've sat there, holding that piece of paper, feeling like you're losing your mind. You're not. What you're experiencing is your nervous system's brilliant, desperate attempt to keep you alive. The problem is, it's still running a program designed for a threat that ended years ago. Stay with me here.

Fibromyalgia isn't a disease in the way we usually think about disease. It's not an infection. It's not a tumor. It's not an autoimmune condition in the classic sense. What it is, is a nervous system that has lost its ability to regulate itself. Your sympathetic nervous system, the one that handles fight or flight, is stuck in the on position. Your parasympathetic system, the one that helps you rest and digest, can't get a word in edgewise. And over time, this constant state of high alert creates real physical changes in your body. Inflammation. Muscle tension. Pain signaling that won't stop.

The science of a stuck nervous system

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed polyvagal theory, spent decades mapping out how our nervous system actually works. And what he found changed everything. It's not just fight or flight. There's a third option, a shutdown response that he calls the dorsal vagal state. This is the freeze response. The collapse. The part of you that goes numb when things get too overwhelming. And here's what's wild. Many people with fibromyalgia are cycling between hyperarousal and shutdown constantly. Their bodies are locked in a loop that they can't escape on their own.

Think about it. When you're in a state of chronic threat, your body releases stress hormones like cortisol and adrenaline. These are meant for short bursts, running from a predator, fighting off an attacker. But when the threat never ends, when it's embedded in your nervous system from childhood abuse or a car accident or a medical trauma, those hormones don't get a chance to clear out. They build up. They wear down your tissues. They sensitize your pain receptors until everything hurts. A gentle touch feels like a burn. A normal day feels like a marathon.

Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing, calls this "incomplete defensive responses." When you're traumatized, your body prepares to fight or flee. But if you couldn't do either, if you had to freeze or submit, that energy gets trapped. It stays in your muscles, in your fascia, in your nervous system. And your body keeps trying to complete the response, but it never can. So it keeps sending pain signals, keeps tensing up, keeps trying to get your attention. It's like a computer that's stuck in a boot loop. The same error keeps appearing, and no amount of restarting fixes it.

A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy. It's not just theory. It's a roadmap for understanding why your body does what it does, and how to start shifting out of survival mode.

The body keeps the score, and the score is pain

Bessel van der Kolk wrote a whole book with that title, and he's right. Your body doesn't just remember trauma in your mind. It remembers in your cells. In your muscles. In your connective tissue. When you experience something overwhelming, your brain stores that memory differently. It doesn't get filed away neatly as a past event. It stays present, alive, ready to trigger at any moment. And your body responds accordingly. Your muscles brace for impact. Your breathing becomes shallow. Your digestion slows down. Your immune system goes on high alert.

Now imagine this happening for years. Decades. Your entire life. The constant tension in your shoulders isn't just stress. It's a held position from the time you had to make yourself small to survive. The burning pain in your lower back isn't just inflammation. It's the place where you held your breath during moments of terror. The fatigue that makes it hard to get out of bed isn't laziness. It's your body's way of saying, "I've been running on empty for so long, I can't anymore."

I know this because I've lived it. I spent years in doctors' offices, getting poked and prodded, being told I was fine. I was told to exercise more, to meditate, to think positive thoughts. And I tried. God, I tried. But none of it worked because no one was addressing the root cause. No one was asking me about what happened to me. No one was looking at my nervous system. They were just treating symptoms, slapping bandages on a wound that needed surgery.

For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger. Levine walks through how animals in the wild shake off trauma, and how we can learn to do the same. It's not about reliving the trauma. It's about letting your body complete the response it never got to finish.

What actually helps? It's not what you think

Here's the part where I have to be honest with you. There's no quick fix. No supplement, no medication, no magic protocol that will make fibromyalgia disappear. If there were, I'd tell you. But what I can tell you is that recovery is possible. It just doesn't look like what the wellness industry sells you. It's not about positive thinking. It's not about "ing" your health. It's about slowly, patiently, gently teaching your nervous system that it's safe now.

This looks different for everyone. For some people, it's learning to track their body's signals without judgment. For others, it's finding a therapist who understands trauma and the body. For many, it's small practices done consistently. A weighted blanket that many find calming for their nervous system is YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs. It provides deep pressure stimulation, which can help activate your parasympathetic nervous system and signal safety to your body.

Breathwork is another piece, but not the kind that tells you to breathe deeply when you're panicking. That can actually make things worse. Instead, it's about lengthening your exhale. A breathing tool that helps slow the exhale for nervous system regulation is Breathing Exercise Tool. It creates gentle resistance on the exhale, which can help calm your vagus nerve and shift you out of fight or flight.

Movement matters too, but not the kind that pushes you past your limits. Gentle, pendular movement. Shaking. Stretching. Things that let your body release stored tension without triggering more pain. And rest. Real rest. Not the kind where you're scrolling on your phone. The kind where you lie down and let your body know it's allowed to let go.

The hard truth you need to hear

I'm not going to tell you this is easy. It's not. Some days, the pain is still there. Some days, the fatigue wins. Some days, I still get angry at the years I lost to a system that didn't know how to help me. But here's what I've learned. Your body is not your enemy. It's not broken. It's not betraying you. It's doing exactly what it was designed to do. It's protecting you. The problem is that the threat it's protecting you from is no longer there. And your body doesn't know that yet.

So your job, and it's a hard one, is to teach it. Not through force. Not through willpower. Through patience. Through curiosity. Through small moments of safety that accumulate over time. Your body needs to learn that it can rest. That it can soften. That it can let go. And it can. It just takes time. And it takes the right kind of support.

You're not crazy. You're not weak. You're not broken. You're a person with a nervous system that's been through too much. And that nervous system can learn new patterns. It can heal. Not perfectly. Not completely. But enough. Enough that the pain becomes manageable. Enough that you can live your life again. Enough that you can start to trust your body instead of fighting it.

Sit with that. Notice what just happened in your body. Did your shoulders drop a little? Did your breath deepen? Did you feel a small opening? That's your body listening. That's your body saying, "Maybe it's safe to try."

  • Waking the Tiger (paid link) - Peter Levine's classic on healing trauma through the body.
